title: Episode 1

jokes.text: The elaborate midnight preparation routine — getting out of bed, drinking wine, showering, shaving everything, digging out Agent Provocateur suspender belt — just to pretend you've been up
jokes.setup: A guy texts at 2am asking to come over; Fleabag has been in bed
jokes.punchline: The absurd escalating chain of effort she goes through purely to maintain the fiction that she was already awake — culminating in 'suspender belt, the whole bit'
jokes.explanation: This scores high because it's a perfect opening gambit — it establishes Fleabag's voice immediately, the escalation is precisely calibrated (wine → shower → shave → Agent Provocateur → door), and the absurdity is grounded in totally recognizable social performance anxiety. 'Agent Provocateur business' is a specific, class-coded detail that does enormous character work. The joke is also structurally elegant: every item in the list is funnier than the last. Earns its 8.5 — it's an exceptional cold open.

jokes.text: She opens the door and performs nonchalant surprise — 'Oh. Hi. Hey.' — after all that preparation
jokes.setup: She has done an insane amount of preparation to pretend she wasn't waiting
jokes.punchline: The performance lands as the most casual, forgettable greeting possible — the gap between effort and outcome is the joke
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the elaborate setup in joke 1. The economy is exceptional — the entire joke is 'Oh. Hi. Hey.' Those five words, delivered after the suspender belt monologue, are devastatingly funny. The gap between the labor and the nonchalant performance is the core comedic mechanism. This is structurally very tight and earns its score.

jokes.text: 'After some pretty standard bouncing, you realise...'
jokes.setup: We are watching/hearing about a sexual encounter
jokes.punchline: 'Pretty standard bouncing' as the breezy, clinical euphemism for sex — the studied casualness of the phrase is funnier than any explicit description could be
jokes.explanation: Three words that do enormous work. 'Pretty standard bouncing' is a masterclass in economy — it's simultaneously deflating, British, unsexy, and completely character-specific. Only Fleabag talks about sex this way. The phrase is immediately quotable and sets up the revelation that follows with perfect tonal contrast. Economy score of 9.5 is justified.

jokes.text: '..he's edging towards your arsehole. But you're drunk and he made the effort to come all the way here, so you let him.'
jokes.setup: Standard sex encounter framing established; 'edging towards your arsehole' is already a bold non-sequitur
jokes.punchline: The justification — 'he made the effort to come all the way here' — is a hilariously mundane social obligation reapplied to an extreme sexual situation. Politeness as the reason for anal sex.
jokes.explanation: This is the joke that announces Fleabag as a genuine work of art. The logic of 'he made the effort to come all the way here' — a phrase so innocuous it could describe letting a salesman in — applied to consenting to anal sex is both shocking and deeply recognizable. It captures the specific female social conditioning around not wasting someone's effort, and it's simultaneously hilarious and quietly devastating. The character integration is near-perfect: this exact calculus is Fleabag's entire psychological profile in miniature. This is a 9+ joke — legitimately exceptional, comparable to the anchor examples.

jokes.text: 'I'm so thrilled.' — the guy breaks the 'you' second-person narration to speak in his own voice
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been narrating in second person ('you let him — he's thrilled')
jokes.punchline: The guy breaks into direct speech — 'I'm so thrilled' — confirming her account with enthusiastic, oblivious sincerity while she (and we) are slightly mortified
jokes.explanation: A structurally elegant moment where the narration breaks and the character confirms exactly what she said with breezy happiness. The comedy comes from the gap between his delight and her detached recounting — and from the formal trick of having the narrated subject speak. It's a small but perfectly timed meta-move that earns the score.

jokes.text: 'Last night was incredible... it was particularly special because I've never managed to actually... up the bum with anyone before.'
jokes.setup: 'Last night was incredible' leads us to expect romantic sentiment; 'particularly special because' deepens that expectation
jokes.punchline: The specific reason it was incredible: a sexual first. '..up the bum with anyone before.' The ellipsis delivery and the phrase 'up the bum' (prim yet explicit) is the comedic engine.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection structure is well-executed: three beats of romantic setup ('incredible,' 'particularly special,' 'I've never managed') all shattered by the anti-climactic but completely literal conclusion. The delayed reveal across the ellipses is well-paced and the phrase 'up the bum' is perfectly chosen — childlike but unambiguous.

jokes.text: 'To be fair, he does have a large penis. And although it's always been a fantasy of mine, I've never found anyone I could do it with.'
jokes.setup: She has just described letting a man she doesn't seem particularly into do something she didn't plan
jokes.punchline: The revelation that she actually wanted this — the entire framing has been as if she were a passive, polite victim, but she was an enthusiastic participant who just narrated it like a social inconvenience
jokes.explanation: This is the retroactive reframe that makes the whole opening sequence sing. She didn't just go along with it out of politeness — she actively wanted it and has been presenting the entire thing as a social ordeal she politely endured. The gap between her narration style and her actual desires is Fleabag's entire character in a nutshell. 'To be fair, he does have a large penis' as a generous aside is wonderfully specific and funny. Earns 8.5.

jokes.text: He thanks her with a 'genuine, earnest' — then says 'Thank you' — and it's 'sort of moving'
jokes.setup: A casual sexual encounter; post-coital morning after
jokes.punchline: He thanks her with sincere emotional gravity for something she's been narrating as a mildly inconvenient social exchange — the earnestness is funnier than any explicit joke could be
jokes.explanation: A smaller, quieter joke that derives its comedy from the emotional mismatch — his genuine warmth versus her detached narration. 'It's sort of moving' is perfectly Fleabag: she won't fully admit the moment affects her. The comedy is subtle and earns a solid 7.5.

jokes.text: 'Do I have a massive arsehole?'
jokes.setup: After the entire encounter — the sex, his emotional morning-after speech, the gentle kiss goodbye — we wait (a long pause follows his leaving) for her existential takeaway
jokes.punchline: The entire morning has led to this single, devastating, absurd self-appraisal question — 'Do I have a massive arsehole?' — delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and one of the great sitcom punchlines. The structural perfection is almost unmatched: a nearly three-minute sustained setup across an entire sexual episode, followed by a pause as he leaves, followed by a pause for her to process everything — and her takeaway is not 'am I lonely,' not 'was that meaningful,' but a completely literal, physical question about her anatomy. It's absurd, it's honest, it's character-defining, it recontextualizes the entire opening sequence, and it's devastatingly funny. The near-perfect structure score is justified: every beat from 00:17 has been building to this. This belongs in the conversation with the greatest sitcom punchlines ever written.

jokes.text: 'Oh, my God. I'm so sorry/really pleased.' — simultaneous reaction to learning she recently broke up with someone
jokes.setup: She reveals she's recently broken up with her boyfriend
jokes.punchline: He says 'I'm so sorry/really pleased' — catching himself mid-condolence and pivoting to honesty in the same breath
jokes.explanation: A sharp little joke. The slash construction — 'I'm so sorry/really pleased' — is economical and honest in a way that captures something true about how people actually process news that is good for them but bad for someone else. Better than average.

jokes.text: 'How the hell did he manage to fuck that up?' — re: Harry breaking up with Fleabag
jokes.setup: She mentions she recently broke up — the implication being her boyfriend ended it or it went wrong somehow
jokes.punchline: His instant confidence that any man who let her go is an idiot — gallant but slightly comic in how quickly he commits
jokes.explanation: A functional flattery-as-comedy beat. Not especially original but serves the scene.

jokes.text: Harry caught masturbating — and his defensive 'I was watching the news' — followed by the interrogation
jokes.setup: Fleabag comes in on Harry; he claims he was watching the news; she demands to know what it was about
jokes.punchline: 'Iraq.' — a single word answer after a building interrogation, delivered too slowly for someone who was genuinely watching
jokes.explanation: The joke is structured well — the political speech playing in the background while he's caught, the confident denial, the escalating interrogation, and finally 'Iraq.' after a very long pause. The beat of silence before his answer is the comedic engine. Clean structure, good economy.

jokes.text: 'Don't say anything. And please don't contact me or turn up at my house drunk in your underwear. It won't work this time.' / 'It will.' / 'I'm taking that posh shampoo.'
jokes.setup: Harry is breaking up with Fleabag in a heartfelt speech about trying to be there for her
jokes.punchline: Three successive punches: (1) 'It won't work this time' implies it worked before — the underwear-drunk-letterbox strategy is an established move; (2) 'It will' — her unfazed, unashamed certainty; (3) 'I'm taking that posh shampoo' — the break-up reduced to petty shampoo theft
jokes.explanation: Three-beat structure that escalates beautifully. 'It won't work this time' is already funny; 'It will' is devastatingly Fleabag (no argument, pure confidence); and 'I'm taking that posh shampoo' completely deflates the emotional register of the break-up scene. The shampoo line is the kind of detail that lands and sticks — petty, specific, funny, and character-defining. Earns its 8.5.

jokes.text: Harry's virtues listed ('cook all the time, run baths, hoover, laugh at all my jokes, great with my family, really fucking affectionate') — followed by 'Sounds like a dickhead. Yeah.'
jokes.setup: She lists every positive quality Harry had — he was essentially perfect
jokes.punchline: 'Sounds like a dickhead.' — and her immediate agreement. The inversion of the expected sympathy response lands perfectly
jokes.explanation: A well-structured ironic reversal — the more perfect the list sounds, the funnier 'Sounds like a dickhead' becomes as a response. Her 'Yeah' completing it without hesitation makes it land harder. The joke carries a genuine sting: she pushed away someone good, and they're both glossing over that. Strong economy.

jokes.text: 'And I'll be sure to treat you like a nasty little bitch.'
jokes.setup: He's been charming and gallant throughout the tube encounter — she gives him her number — a romantic moment
jokes.punchline: His signed-off parting line is 'And I'll be sure to treat you like a nasty little bitch' — completely detonating the romantic register
jokes.explanation: The line is structurally perfect because it follows a string of entirely proper, even sweet behaviour and explodes it. It also retroactively suggests she's attracted to something darker than the surface suggests — which is real character information delivered as a punchline. The flustered 'Er, that was a joke. Oh, no, I know' follow-up is the correct comic button. Economy is very high — one line does enormous tonal work.

jokes.text: Bank manager's aside about not supporting women-led businesses 'since the... Sexual harassment case.'
jokes.setup: He's trying to explain their current cautious approach to women-led businesses
jokes.punchline: He can't quite bring himself to say 'sexual harassment case' — Fleabag supplies the phrase for him — and then he repeats it, confirming it as if she'd helped him with a word he couldn't find
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of 'we haven't had the opportunity to support many — any — women-led businesses since the...' and her supplying 'Sexual harassment case' and him confirming 'The sexual harassment case. Yes.' is a very clean cringe comedy beat. The hypocritical institutional self-awareness (they can't help women because they harassed women) is the dark core.

jokes.text: Fleabag's top comes undone during the loan meeting — she insists it's an accident; 'I'm not trying to shag you! Look at yourself!'
jokes.setup: A professional loan meeting; the bank has a recent sexual harassment history; her top comes undone accidentally
jokes.punchline: The situation spirals — she insists it's innocent, he insists it isn't, and she escalates to 'I'm not trying to shag you! Look at yourself!' — a blurt of honest contempt that destroys any remaining goodwill
jokes.explanation: The cringe escalation is well-constructed — the stakes are real (she needs this loan desperately), which makes the disaster funnier. 'Look at yourself!' is the key line: the perfect wrong thing to say, delivered with complete sincerity. The character integration is strong — only Fleabag would blurt this.

jokes.text: Fleabag introduces her sister to camera: 'She's uptight and beautiful and probably anorexic, but clothes look awesome on her, so...'
jokes.setup: Introduction of a new character — the sister
jokes.punchline: '...clothes look awesome on her, so...' — the trailing-off acceptance of someone whose terrible qualities are forgiven for aesthetic reasons. The 'so...' is doing all the work.
jokes.explanation: A strong character introduction that does double work: establishes the sister AND establishes something about Fleabag's values (she resents her sister but also envies/accepts her). The 'so...' as a trailing acceptance is economically great.

jokes.text: 'Had to do a flash poo in Pret.'
jokes.setup: She arrives slightly late to the feminist lecture; her sister asks 'You're almost late.'
jokes.punchline: 'Had to do a flash poo in Pret.' — completely unfiltered, delivered as a matter-of-fact explanation
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'flash poo' is a specific, British, perfectly calibrated piece of language that only this character would deploy in this context. The casualness is total — she's at a feminist lecture, meeting her high-powered sister, and she leads with this. Deeply character-specific. 'Flash poo in Pret' is also extremely quotable. High character integration score justified.

jokes.text: Sister horrified: 'Did you wash your hands?' — 'Of course not.' — [pause] — 'Of course I washed my hands.'
jokes.setup: The flash poo revelation prompts the sister to ask if she washed her hands
jokes.punchline: She says 'Of course not' — a beat — then 'Of course I washed my hands. It's not like I grew up without a mother.' The correction is funny because: (a) she clearly enjoyed the fake-out, and (b) the justification 'It's not like I grew up without a mother' implies they did lose their mother, which is the episode's dark undertow
jokes.explanation: The triple-beat structure — 'Of course not' / [sister reacts] / 'Of course I washed my hands' — is already good. But the addition of 'It's not like I grew up without a mother' is where it becomes exceptional: it's played as a deadpan non-sequitur but lands as dark foreshadowing that their mother is dead. The joke works on pure comedy AND plants a grenade of pathos. This is Fleabag at its most characteristic.

jokes.text: 'Of course I washed my hands. It's not like I grew up without a mother.'
jokes.setup: She's just said 'of course not' (didn't wash hands); Claire is horrified
jokes.punchline: She reveals she did wash her hands — but the justification is 'it's not like I grew up without a mother,' which lands as both a recovery and a sudden dark reveal that they ARE, in fact, motherless daughters
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional and one of the episode's best-crafted jokes because it operates on two levels simultaneously. On the surface: she IS washing her hands, she was pranking Claire. But the punchline — 'it's not like I grew up without a mother' — lands differently because we've just been told (or will soon be told) that they ARE motherless. It's funny as a straight comeback, and devastating in retrospect. This is exactly the kind of layered writing that defines Fleabag at its best.

jokes.text: 'Heard from Dad? No.' — followed immediately by the talking-head about their father's coping strategies
jokes.setup: Quick exchange: 'Heard from Dad? No.'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's VO: 'Dad's way of coping with two motherless daughters was to buy us tickets to feminist lectures, start fucking our godmother and eventually stop calling.'
jokes.explanation: The three-item escalation ('feminist lectures... start fucking our godmother... eventually stop calling') is masterfully constructed. Each item is funnier and darker than the last, and they don't really connect logically — which is exactly right, because they're the random, inadequate gestures of a man who doesn't know what to do with grief. The word 'motherless' confirms what was implied in joke 23. This is dense, economical, and builds a family portrait in one sentence. Earns 8.7.

jokes.text: 'You look tired.' / 'Thanks. I've been sleeping really well recently.'
jokes.setup: Claire makes an observation disguised as a dig
jokes.punchline: Fleabag responds as if it were a compliment, or refuses to engage with the implied insult — 'I've been sleeping really well recently' as a non-sequitur that neutralizes the intended wound
jokes.explanation: A beautifully economical sisterly exchange. The ambiguity of Fleabag's response is its strength — is she mocking the dig, or genuinely misreading it, or making a self-deprecating point? The uncertainty is funnier than a direct comeback would be.

jokes.text: Claire asks Fleabag to take her coat off; Fleabag refuses — the coat is concealing the stolen top
jokes.setup: Joke 26 established the top is hidden under the coat
jokes.punchline: When asked to remove the coat, a flat 'No.' — the reason known only to us and Fleabag
jokes.explanation: The audience is in on the secret; the suspense of whether the top will be noticed is a sustained cringe element. 'No.' as the total response is economically perfect.

jokes.text: Claire asks about the cafe; Fleabag cuts her off — 'I don't want to talk about it yet.' / 'We won't talk, then. Fine.' / 'Hair looks nice.' / 'Oh, fuck off.'
jokes.setup: Claire tries to ask a practical question; Fleabag shuts it down; Claire tries a compliment
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, fuck off.' — her response to a genuine compliment. The transition from 'We won't talk, then' to a deliberate olive branch compliment to being told to fuck off is a precise comedy of sisterhood
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of the exchange is good — the rapid topic-closing followed by an attempted softness being rejected immediately. Very true to a specific kind of sibling relationship. 'Oh, fuck off' as a response to 'Hair looks nice' is the right button.

jokes.text: Fleabag's internal monologue about asking Claire for money — building to 'I'm just going to ask her. I'm just going to ask her. I'm just going to come...' — 'Do you need to borrow money? No!'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has established she's run out of money and needs to ask her sister — 'The only thing harder than having to tell your super-high-powered, perfect, anorexic, rich super-sister...'
jokes.punchline: She builds to a commitment — 'I'm just going to ask her' x3 — Claire jumps in first with 'Do you need to borrow money?' — and Fleabag's instant reflexive 'No!' defeats the entire purpose
jokes.explanation: The comedic structure is precise: the building internal repetition ('I'm just going to ask her') creates expectation, which is subverted when Claire gets there first — and then obliterated when Fleabag's pride kicks in with an instant 'No!' The joke is also character-defining: her pride prevents the practical action she needs to take. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'So business is good, then? Yeah. It's good. It's really good. It's really, really good. Yeah, it's really good. Sounds like it's really good. It is.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just refused to admit she needs money; both know the business is failing
jokes.punchline: The repetition escalates into absurdity — both sisters performing normalcy with increasing desperation, Claire's 'Sounds like it's really good' the dry needle in the balloon
jokes.explanation: The rhythmic escalation of 'really good' is a cringe comedy classic structure — the verbal equivalent of two people refusing to acknowledge an elephant. 'Sounds like it's really good' from Claire is the satirical pin; she knows exactly what's happening. The exchange works well and would score a strong laugh from an audience.

jokes.text: 'Don't get drunk and shit in your sink again.' / 'When are you going to stop bringing that up?' / 'When you do something better.'
jokes.setup: Claire warns Fleabag not to do the drunk-letterbox thing again
jokes.punchline: Escalates from letterbox-screaming to 'shit in your sink' — another level of rock bottom behavior; and then 'When you do something better' as the logic: the sink-shitting holds the record until something worse replaces it
jokes.explanation: Three-beat exchange that becomes near-perfect comedy. The revelation of the sink-shitting is already shocking and funny; 'When are you going to stop bringing that up?' is exactly the wrong response (she's defending it by implying it should have a statute of limitations); and 'When you do something better' is the comic pinnacle — the terrifying implication that there's a ranked list and the sink incident currently tops it. Structure and economy scores are high because those three lines are maximally efficient. Legitimately close to the 8.0 anchor level and possibly above it.

jokes.text: 'I have two degrees, a husband and a Burberry coat. You shat in a sink.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag challenges why Claire keeps bringing up the sink incident
jokes.punchline: Claire lists her credentials ('two degrees, a husband and a Burberry coat') against Fleabag's counter-credential: 'You shat in a sink.' The Burberry coat as a status symbol placed alongside degrees and marriage, and then demolished by a single toilet fact.
jokes.explanation: This is the second candidate for best joke in the episode. The structure is formally perfect: a list of three ascending status markers (degrees — respectable, husband — respectable, Burberry coat — slightly comic in how specific it is) versus one incontrovertible counter-fact. The asymmetry is the joke's engine — the coat is already a bit funny as a status symbol, but then 'You shat in a sink' doesn't argue against any of it, just renders all of it irrelevant. It's also deeply character-specific: this is how these two sisters argue. Quotability is perfect — this line is immediately memorable. Earns its high scores.

jokes.text: 'I swear there are pants that give you thrush. What are yours made from? Don't know. I need to get sexy pants. I hate my body, I hate my body, I hate my body, I hate my body.'
jokes.setup: They're getting changed/ready for a party — first introduction of Boo
jokes.punchline: The internal monologue goes from thrush logistics to 'I hate my body' x4 — the self-loathing is funny not because self-loathing is funny but because of the escalating repetition and the way she cycles from practical concern to existential despair in seconds
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'I hate my body' functions as escalation-through-volume — the joke is that she's not saying it once but four times in a row, like a broken record. The context shift (from 'what are pants made of' to 'I hate my body x4') is the comedic engine. Functional, specific, character-grounded.

jokes.text: Boo: 'Oh my God. Definitely not. That does nothing for you.' — Fleabag: 'I hate that.' / 'What?' / 'These are MY clothes, Boo. I've been wearing these all day.'
jokes.setup: Boo critiques what Fleabag is wearing; we assume it's an outfit she's trying on
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Fleabag is wearing her own clothes that she's been in all day — meaning Boo just unknowingly told her she looks terrible in her actual outfit
jokes.explanation: A well-structured misdirection: we assume Boo is critiquing a proposed outfit, and the reveal that it's what she's already wearing makes the insult suddenly real and irretrievable. The friendship dynamic is funny and recognizable.

jokes.text: 'I'll buy you sexy pants! I've been so many places today!' — Boo's attempted consolation about the underwear/outfit situation
jokes.setup: Boo is trying to make it up to Fleabag for insulting her real outfit
jokes.punchline: 'I'll buy you sexy pants! I've been so many places today!' — the non-sequitur energy of the second sentence is funny; it makes no clear sense as a reason or consolation but has the manic logic of a genuine friend trying to fix things
jokes.explanation: Boo's manic energy is funny and character-establishing. The non-sequitur of 'I've been so many places today' as a reason for buying pants is absurdist and character-specific, but slightly underpowered on its own.

jokes.text: 'Ugh! I mean, you really shouldn't wear such cheap materials. They don't let your fanny breathe.'
jokes.setup: The outfit/pants conversation; Boo has been apologetic
jokes.punchline: She pivots from consolation to a second critique — 'cheap materials... don't let your fanny breathe' — delivered as practical advice, which makes it worse
jokes.explanation: A solid button to the outfit scene — Boo is constitutionally unable to stop giving unhelpful advice. The specificity of 'fanny breathe' and the clinical authority with which she delivers it is funny.

jokes.text: The Women Speak facilitator: 'Opening women's mouths since 1998.'
jokes.setup: Introduction to a feminist lecture series
jokes.punchline: 'Opening women's mouths since 1998' — an unintentionally (or intentionally?) sexual double entendre as a feminist organization's tagline
jokes.explanation: The double entendre is well-placed in context — a feminist organization whose tagline accidentally (or satirically) sounds sexual. The economy is high; it's one short phrase that does double work. Good satirical bite.

jokes.text: The 'raise your hand if you'd trade five years of your life for the perfect body' bit — the camera (implied) holds on the women in the room, building suspense
jokes.setup: The facilitator poses a serious feminist question — a test of values
jokes.punchline: The very long pause (from 14:28 to ~15:04 is 36 seconds of silence) implies that almost everyone's hand goes up — or worse, the expected 'nobody would' doesn't happen. The comedy is entirely in the pause and the implied hands rising.
jokes.explanation: This is a pure visual/silence gag that can only be analyzed from inference, but it's a structural standout. The 36-second hold implies hands going up, which would mean: women at a feminist lecture would sell five years of their lives for a body they love. The joke is simultaneously funny and devastating — it makes a genuine point about women's relationship with their bodies while getting a laugh from the uncomfortable honesty. High structure score because the pure comedy comes from the silence rather than any line.

jokes.text: 'Won't you get cold? Nah, I've got really hairy nipples.'
jokes.setup: Boo is apparently wearing something revealing to a party
jokes.punchline: 'I've got really hairy nipples' as a practical reason not to get cold — delivered with the same casual authority as all of Boo's pronouncements
jokes.explanation: Pure Boo: the complete non-sequitur delivered as practical insulation logic. The specificity of 'hairy nipples' as a thermal defense is absurd and deeply funny. High character integration because this exact brand of blunt body-talk is entirely Boo. Immediately quotable. One of the episode's tightest jokes.

jokes.text: Fleabag's double-take reaction to 'I've got really hairy nipples' — her 'What?' and the implied look to camera
jokes.setup: Boo delivers 'hairy nipples' with total nonchalance
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's stunned 'What?' — and presumably a look to camera
jokes.explanation: A reaction beat that amplifies joke 40. The 'What?' is funny because it establishes that even Fleabag — who is telling us about anal sex — is momentarily floored by Boo. Functional comedy extension but not a joke in its own right.

jokes.text: Fleabag receives an attempted hug and reacts with terror: 'Jesus! A fucking hug! That's terrifying. Never do that again.'
jokes.setup: After the party, Boo tries to give Fleabag a hug
jokes.punchline: Fleabag treats a hug as a physical assault — 'That's terrifying. Never do that again.' The disproportionate response to physical affection is a character reveal played as comedy
jokes.explanation: A joke that does double work: it's funny on its surface (extreme reaction to a hug) but also reveals Fleabag's discomfort with genuine physical tenderness — she can handle anonymous sex but not an affectionate hug from a friend. The comedy and the character insight arrive simultaneously. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'Do you want to go for a drink?' — Claire doesn't answer; Fleabag repeats it after a pause; still nothing
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks her sister for a drink
jokes.punchline: The pause, the repetition, and the implied non-answer — Claire either didn't hear or is pointedly not responding, and either way the silence is the joke
jokes.explanation: A small but well-observed moment of social awkwardness. The comedy is in the timing — asking once, waiting, asking again into the void. Character-revealing without being a major joke.

jokes.text: Fleabag to camera: 'My sister blows glass. She has done for a long time.' Followed immediately by 'I've never been in a fight. Well, I've been in a fight. Never been punched in the face. I've been punched in the leg. And someone once threw some punch in my face.'
jokes.setup: Talking-head style observation about the bar they're in or their situation
jokes.punchline: The rapid self-correction sequence — 'I've never been in a fight. Well... never been punched in the face. I've been punched in the leg. Someone once threw some punch in my face.' — ending on the pun ('punch' the drink / 'punch' the action)
jokes.explanation: The escalating self-corrections are beautifully constructed — each qualification undercuts the previous statement ('never been in a fight' → 'been in a fight' → 'never punched in the face' → 'punched in the leg' → 'someone threw punch in my face'). The final beat works both as a pun and as an escalation landing. The descent from 'I'm not a fighter' to 'someone once threw punch-the-drink at me' is funny and character-specific. Economy is high.

jokes.text: Fleabag's talking-head: 'My sister blows glass. She has done for a long time.' / 'I've never been in a fight. Well, I've been in a fight. Never been punched in the face. I've been punched in the leg. And someone once threw some punch in my face.'
jokes.setup: She's introducing her date/companion in a talking-head
jokes.punchline: The fight/punch/punch-in-face wordplay escalates into 'someone once threw some punch in my face' — the drink pun is the payoff
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay construction: fight → punched in the face → punched in the leg (escalating embarrassment) → 'punch' (the drink) in the face. The structure is a well-built word game that rewards attention. Very quotable and pleasingly absurd.

jokes.text: 'Same again, while I'm up? Or perhaps, like, a little cocky-tail? Or, like, a nice shot?'
jokes.setup: A man Fleabag is on some kind of date with offers her a drink
jokes.punchline: 'Cocky-tail' — a barely-concealed 'cocktail/cock' pun — delivered with studied casualness; the specific style of this guy is established immediately as cringe-inducingly flirtatious
jokes.explanation: The 'cocky-tail' pun is itself weak and cringe-worthy, but that might be the point — it's characterizing a type of man. The comedy is more in Fleabag's reaction than the pun itself. Functional but not strong.

jokes.text: She says 'Or we could just go back to mine' — he hedges, she repeats, he hedges again, she escalates — until: 'OK, what the fuck is your problem?'
jokes.setup: She's trying to take things forward; he keeps deflecting with excuses about work
jokes.punchline: The escalation of his increasingly implausible hedges and her increasing frustration climaxing in 'What the fuck is your problem?' — then reversed when she says it's nothing and he calls her pathetic
jokes.explanation: The escalation of rejection is well-handled and the reversal (she's the one who becomes difficult when he finally commits) is a good character beat. 'You're pathetic' and 'Y-You dropped this' are funny tonal shifts. Not a standout but competent.

jokes.text: After the bar argument — Fleabag alone with a stranger, drunk — he asks 'Are you OK?' and she asks it back. 'Sad face.' 'I'm fine.'
jokes.setup: She's clearly not OK — crying, drunk, alone after a failed interaction
jokes.punchline: 'Sad face.' — the stranger's verbal labeling of her expression, as if naming it rather than addressing it; her 'I'm fine' completing the absurd exchange
jokes.explanation: The verbal labeling of an emotion as a substitute for addressing it is funny and quietly devastating. 'Sad face' as a stranger's response to obvious distress is both absurdist and accurate to how people deflect. The 'I'm fine' completes it. Good economy.

jokes.text: The old man at the bar invites Fleabag home: 'Do you want to come home with me?' 'What?! No way! You naughty boy.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been comforted by an older man at the bar
jokes.punchline: 'You naughty boy.' — her response transforms her from vulnerable to flirtatiously maternal, delivered with a kind of affectionate contempt that is both funny and slightly alarming
jokes.explanation: The tonal pivot from vulnerable to 'You naughty boy' is sharp. The phrase itself is perfectly chosen — it's what you'd say to a mischievous child, which makes it both dismissive and oddly fond when applied to a lecherous old man. Character-specific reaction.

jokes.text: Boo's song: '# Another lunch break another portion / Another piece of cake / Another two Fuck it - 20 / Cigarettes / And we're happy / So happy / To be modern women. #'
jokes.setup: They're drunk outside, Fleabag has given up on the night
jokes.punchline: Boo improvises/sings a deliberately awful but honest song about being a modern woman — the self-awareness of '20 cigarettes and we're happy / so happy' as a parody of empowerment narratives
jokes.explanation: The song is funny for its mid-lyric self-correction ('two Fuck it — 20 cigarettes') and the sardonic 'So happy / To be modern women' which simultaneously sends up and captures the show's own thesis. Boo as a character is crystallized here. Good character integration.

jokes.text: 'Let's never ask anyone for anything. They don't get it.' / 'Deal. Deal. Fuck it.'
jokes.setup: End of the night; both have failed at their respective endeavors
jokes.punchline: 'Fuck it.' as the shared philosophy — delivered as a statement of principle, the double 'Deal' and then the resigned 'Fuck it' is funny in its abrupt finality
jokes.explanation: A quiet, character-grounded joke about two people agreeing to embrace nihilism as a solution. The comedy is in the earnestness with which 'Fuck it' is delivered as a pact. Functional.

jokes.text: 'This is totally fine.' — delivered to no one, in what is apparently not fine circumstances
jokes.setup: She's let herself into her dad's house at 2am
jokes.punchline: 'This is totally fine.' — pure ironic self-reassurance delivered with the confidence of someone who knows it isn't
jokes.explanation: A small ironic beat that works through understatement. Not especially crafted but well-placed in the episode's emotional arc.

jokes.text: Fleabag's self-diagnosis: 'I have a horrible feeling that I'm a greedy, perverted, selfish, apathetic, cynical, depraved, morally bankrupt woman who can't even call herself a feminist.'
jokes.setup: She's unburdening herself to her incapable father after the entire disastrous day
jokes.punchline: The list escalates magnificently — 'greedy, perverted, selfish, apathetic, cynical, depraved, morally bankrupt' — only to land on the kicker: 'who can't even call herself a feminist.' The final item doesn't match the register of the others; it's simultaneously smaller and, to her, worse.
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. The list structure is perfect: seven escalating condemnations that anyone might use as self-flagellation, culminating in 'who can't even call herself a feminist' — which after 'morally bankrupt' sounds like adding 'and I returned a library book late.' The bathos is precisely calibrated. But then it reframes: in context of the episode and the show, she's not joking — this is genuinely what she fears. The comedy and the pathos arrive simultaneously. Strong character integration: only Fleabag in this world would rank 'can't call herself a feminist' with 'depraved.' Earns 9.0.

jokes.text: 'Well, er... You get all that from your mother.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just delivered a devastating self-condemnation (joke 53); Dad has been completely useless throughout
jokes.punchline: 'You get all that from your mother.' — the worst possible response, simultaneously a deflection, an insult to a dead woman, a joke at Fleabag's expense, and a revelation about the family's dynamic; delivered with apparent casual sincerity
jokes.explanation: This is arguably the best line in the episode. After seven minutes of Dad failing to respond to his daughter's pain, he finally speaks — and his response is 'You get all that from your mother.' In five words: (1) he dismisses her entire self-condemnation; (2) he blames a dead woman; (3) he reveals something of his own grief/resentment about the mother; (4) he confirms why he's been useless ('I don't know how to help you because I couldn't help her'); (5) it's darkly, shockingly funny. The economy is perfect — ten words doing the work of a therapy session. The setup has been built for 20 minutes and the payoff is proportionate. Only narrowly edged by joke 9 structurally but arguably its emotional equal. This is a 9.5.

jokes.text: 'Good one.' — Fleabag's response to 'You get all that from your mother'
jokes.setup: Her father has just said something devastating; she processes it
jokes.punchline: 'Good one.' — completely deadpan acknowledgment. She's not attacking him; she's conceding the dark humor while also being wounded. The two-word response is everything.
jokes.explanation: 'Good one.' in response to 'You get all that from your mother' is the correct response — the only response — and its correctness is what makes it funny. It acknowledges the joke, refuses to make anything of it, and closes the exchange with a kind of elegance. High economy and character integration scores justified: only Fleabag would respond this way.

jokes.text: 'Good one.' / 'Er, I'm going to call you a cab, darling. And, er... please don't go upstairs.'
jokes.setup: Dad has just delivered the 'you get all that from your mother' line
jokes.punchline: 'Good one' acknowledges the joke, then 'please don't go upstairs' reintroduces the godmother situation — suggesting she's up there — with no further comment
jokes.explanation: 'Please don't go upstairs' is a perfect deadpan implication — the godmother is up there. The casual way Dad manages this is very funny, and Fleabag's 'good one' as an acknowledgment of the mother joke before immediately pivoting to the next embarrassment is well-structured.

jokes.text: 'Please don't go upstairs.' — Dad's quiet warning after booking her a cab
jokes.setup: He's booking a cab and sees her to the door; there's something upstairs he doesn't want her to encounter
jokes.punchline: The understated 'Please don't go upstairs' — the implication (the Godmother is upstairs) delivered without explanation, which is funnier than any confirmation
jokes.explanation: The callbacks to the godmother pay off here without explanation — 'please don't go upstairs' is funnier than naming what's up there. The economy of implication is precise. The dark situation (Dad is managing his grieving daughter while also managing his secret affair with her godmother) is compressed into five words. Strong.

jokes.text: 'To be fair, she's not an evil stepmother. She's just a cunt.'
jokes.setup: Introduction of the Godmother character
jokes.punchline: 'She's just a cunt.' — after the apparent generosity of 'to be fair, she's not an evil stepmother,' the word 'cunt' arrives as a very specific British escalation that is simultaneously the most and least diplomatic way to frame it
jokes.explanation: The structure is perfect: 'to be fair' signals a generous assessment coming; 'she's not an evil stepmother' is the apparent concession; 'she's just a cunt' demolishes it. In British English, 'cunt' as the endpoint of a seemingly balanced evaluation is specifically funnier than it would be elsewhere. The brevity is also character-defining: Fleabag doesn't over-explain, she lands and moves on. High economy score justified.

jokes.text: 'To be fair, she's not an evil stepmother. She's just a cunt.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is narrating her feelings about the Godmother who is now with her father
jokes.punchline: The careful, almost legalistic distinction between 'evil stepmother' and 'cunt' — delivered as a genuine effort to be fair — is hilarious in its precision
jokes.explanation: The structure is perfect: 'to be fair' signals an attempt at fairness and nuance, 'she's not an evil stepmother' establishes a baseline of reasonableness, and 'she's just a cunt' demolishes it with maximum precision. The word 'just' is doing extraordinary work — it implies 'cunt' is less bad than 'evil stepmother,' which is obviously untrue, but which makes the sentence funnier. Economy is perfect. Extremely quotable. This is an anchor-level joke.

jokes.text: The Godmother's sculpture explanation — 'She's actually an expression of how women are subtle warriors' — and Fleabag's 'Tits.'
jokes.setup: Godmother explains her female sculpture's philosophical meaning
jokes.punchline: Fleabag cuts to '..Tits.' mid-sentence; Godmother continues with 'innate femininity' while Fleabag inserts 'Tits don't get you anywhere these days. Trust me.'
jokes.explanation: The double punchline structure is good: first 'Tits.' as an interruption/substitution for what Godmother is clearly pointing at; then 'Tits don't get you anywhere these days. Trust me.' as a darkly autobiographical aside. The callback to the bank scene is implied but not stated. Strong character integration — this is Fleabag being simultaneously reductive and vulnerable.

jokes.text: 'Tits don't get you anywhere these days. Trust me.'
jokes.setup: The Godmother has pivoted to talking about how femininity is 'very valuable'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's flat 'Trust me' after 'tits don't get you anywhere' is a wry self-report from someone who has clearly tried using their appearance instrumentally, with mixed results
jokes.explanation: 'Trust me' at the end is the key — it positions her as an authority on failed seduction by appearance, which connects neatly with the bank scene and the opening. Good character integration.

jokes.text: 'How much? Thousands. Oh. Can I have it? No.'
jokes.setup: Godmother mentions the sculpture is very valuable
jokes.punchline: Fleabag immediately pivots to 'Can I have it?' — completely serious, given she needs money. Godmother's flat 'No.' is the correct button.
jokes.explanation: A neat callback to the money subplot. 'Can I have it?' is the kind of desperate, direct request only Fleabag would make openly; 'No.' is the perfect response. Good economy.

jokes.text: Godmother reveals the second sculpture: 'Oh, er...my self-portrait.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks about a second piece
jokes.punchline: 'My self-portrait.' — the comedy relies almost entirely on the visual of what this self-portrait apparently looks like; the pause and 'Oh.' from Fleabag confirms it's something notable
jokes.explanation: A visual gag entirely dependent on what we see on screen — the self-portrait is presumably unflattering, abstract, or otherwise alarming. The 'Oh.' from Fleabag is the comedy button. Without the image it partially loses its punch in transcript form, but the structure is clearly there.

jokes.text: 'Please look after yourself. You really do look ghastly, darling.'
jokes.setup: Parting words as Fleabag leaves
jokes.punchline: 'Please look after yourself' sounds like genuine care; 'You really do look ghastly, darling' makes clear it is also an observation and an insult, delivered with perfect passive-aggressive warmth
jokes.explanation: The 'darling' at the end of 'ghastly' is character-defining for the Godmother — the weapon dressed as affection. Clean structure with the care/insult pivot.

jokes.text: Cab driver asks about the cafe: 'Shoot.' / 'I opened the cafe with my friend Boo. Cute name. Yeah. Yeah, she's dead now.'
jokes.setup: A late-night cab ride; driver asks about her funny business story in a casual, warm way
jokes.punchline: 'Cute name. Yeah.' — the driver does what the audience does, tracks along pleasantly — 'Yeah, she's dead now.' The casualness of the delivery is the entire joke; the tonal whiplash is enormous
jokes.explanation: The construction is devastating. The driver's 'Cute name' creates a tiny warm beat — we're all just enjoying a nice story about a cafe and a friend called Boo. 'Yeah, she's dead now.' arrives with the same tone as everything else. The casualness IS the joke — and the trauma. This is the episode's emotional core delivered as a punchline. The driver's 'Cute name / Yeah' is perfectly placed as a misdirection beat. Earns 9.0.

jokes.text: Boo's death: 'She accidentally killed herself. Wasn't her intention, but it wasn't a total accident... She decided to walk into a busy cycle lane, wanting to get tangled in a bike — break a finger, maybe. As it turns out, bikes go fast and flip you into the road. Three people died. She was such a dick.'
jokes.setup: She's explaining Boo's death — the story has the form of a funny anecdote but the content of a tragedy
jokes.punchline: The escalation — 'break a finger, maybe' → 'bikes go fast and flip you into the road' → 'Three people died' → 'She was such a dick.' — is structurally perfect dark comedy. The final assessment ('she was such a dick') is simultaneously the worst and the most honest thing to say, and it's how genuine grief sometimes works.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's emotional and comedic summit. The structure is formally perfect — 'accidentally killed herself / wasn't her intention / but not a total accident' establishes the paradox in three elegant beats; then the practical plan ('break a finger, maybe') makes Boo real and foolish and human; then 'bikes go fast' as the most devastating understatement of consequence; then 'Three people died' which wasn't in the plan at all; then 'She was such a dick.' The entire episode has been building to the revelation that Boo is dead, and when it arrives it arrives as both a joke and an act of grief. 'She was such a dick' is the way real people talk about people they loved who did something terrible and stupid. It's not cruel — it's intimate. This is among the finest closing lines of any sitcom pilot. Perfect craft scores are justified: this is not just a joke but a piece of writing that will be remembered.

jokes.text: 'So, yeah. Kind of on my own.'
jokes.setup: She has just delivered the full account of Boo's death
jokes.punchline: She returns to the original question — 'kind of on my own' with the cafe — with the most controlled possible understatement. The 'kind of' is doing enormous work.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the very beginning of the cab conversation closes the episode perfectly. 'Kind of on my own' was said earlier as a light hedge; now we know what it means. The 'kind of' — still present — is the joke and the heartbreak simultaneously. Only Fleabag hedges her grief with 'kind of.' This is the perfect button to the episode and earns its high score for economy and character integration above all.

tmdb_overview: Angry, pervy, outrageous and hilarious, Fleabag arrives with a bang, as she spins through the city grasping at anyone and anything that might keep her head above water.
